concepts.title: Natural Numbers, Whole Numbers, Integers and Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: The number system includes Natural numbers (N: 1, 2, 3...), Whole numbers (W: includes 0), Integers (Z: includes negatives), and Rational numbers (Q: p/q form where p, q are integers and q ≠ 0). 'Rational' comes from 'ratio' and Q from 'quotient'.
examples: Example 1: Every whole number is NOT a natural number (0 is the exception); Every integer IS a rational number (m = m/1).
Example 2: Five rational numbers between 1 and 2 can be found by writing them as 6/6 and 12/6, giving 7/6, 8/6, 9/6, 10/6, 11/6.
Infinitely many rational numbers exist between any two given rational numbers.

concepts.title: Irrational Numbers and Number Line Representation
concepts.definition: Irrational numbers cannot be written in the form p/q (e.g., √2, √3, π). Real numbers (R) are the collection of all rational and irrational numbers. Every real number corresponds to a unique point on the number line.
formulae: Pythagoras Theorem: OB = √(1² + 1²) = √2 (Used to locate surds geometrically)
examples: Example 3: To locate √2, construct a unit square OABC on the number line; the diagonal OB is √2. Draw an arc with radius OB to mark P on the line.
Example 4: To locate √3, construct unit perpendicular BD on OB; the hyphenuse OD is √3.
Classroom Activity: Constructing the 'square root spiral' for √2, √3, √4...

concepts.title: Real Numbers and their Decimal Expansions
concepts.definition: The decimal expansion of a rational number is either terminating (e.g., 7/8 = 0.875) or non-terminating recurring (e.g., 10/3 = 3.333...). For irrational numbers, it is non-terminating non-recurring.
examples: Case (i): Remainder becomes zero (Terminating). Case (ii): Remainder never becomes zero (Repeating/Recurring).
Example 7: 0.333... = 0.3̅ can be expressed as 1/3.
Example 8: 1.2727... = 1.27̅ can be expressed as 14/11.
Example 9: 0.23535... = 0.235̅ can be expressed as 233/990.
Irrational example: 0.10110111011110... is non-terminating and non-recurring.

concepts.title: Operations on Real Numbers and Identities
concepts.definition: The sum, difference, product, and quotient of a rational and an irrational number are irrational. However, operations between two irrationals can result in a rational or irrational number.
formulae: √(ab) = √a√b
(a + √b)(a - √b) = a² - b

concepts.title: Rationalising the Denominator
concepts.definition: The process of converting an expression with a radical in the denominator into an equivalent expression with a rational denominator.
examples: Example 17: 1/(2+√3) is rationalised by multiplying by (2-√3)/(2-√3) to get 2-√3.
Example 18: 5/(√3-√5) rationalised gives (-5/2)(√3+√5).
Example 19: 1/(7+3√2) rationalised gives (7-3√2)/31.

concepts.title: Laws of Exponents for Real Numbers
concepts.definition: Extension of exponential laws to include rational exponents for a positive real number 'a'.
formulae: a^p . a^q = a^(p+q)
(a^p)^q = a^(pq)
a^p / a^q = a^(p-q)
a^p . b^p = (ab)^p
a^(m/n) = (ⁿ√a)ᵐ = ⁿ√(aᵐ)

concepts.title: Definition and Degree of a Polynomial
concepts.definition: A polynomial in one variable x is an algebraic expression of the form a_n x^n + ... + a_1 x + a_0, where a_n ≠ 0 and exponents of x are whole numbers. The highest power of x is the 'degree'. The degree of a non-zero constant polynomial is 0, and the degree of the zero polynomial is not defined.
examples: x³ - x² + 4x + 7 is a polynomial in one variable x (coefficients are 1, -1, 4, 7).
√x + 3 is NOT a polynomial as the exponent (1/2) is not a whole number.
Example 1: Degree of x⁵ - x⁴ + 3 is 5; Degree of 2 is 0.

concepts.title: Types of Polynomials by Terms and Degree
concepts.definition: Polynomials categorized by terms: Monomial (1 term), Binomial (2 terms), Trinomial (3 terms). Categorized by degree: Linear (degree 1), Quadratic (degree 2), Cubic (degree 3).
formulae: Linear: ax + b (a ≠ 0)
Quadratic: ax² + bx + c (a ≠ 0)
Cubic: ax³ + bx² + cx + d (a ≠ 0)
examples: Monomials: 2x, 5x³, -5x²; Binomials: x+1, x²-x; Trinomials: x+x²+π.
Linear: 4x+5; Quadratic: 5-y², 6-y-y²; Cubic: 4x³, 6-x³.

concepts.title: Zeroes of a Polynomial
concepts.definition: A zero of a polynomial p(x) is a number 'c' such that p(c) = 0. A non-zero constant polynomial has no zero. Every real number is a zero of the zero polynomial.
examples: Example 2: Value of p(x) = 5x² - 3x + 7 at x = 1 is p(1) = 9.
Example 3: -2 is a zero of x+2 because p(-2) = 0.
Example 4: Zero of 2x+1 is found by solving 2x+1=0 (x = -1/2).
Observations: (i) Zero need not be 0. (ii) 0 may be a zero. (iii) Linear polynomial has unique zero.

concepts.title: Remainder Theorem
concepts.definition: If a polynomial p(x) is divided by (x − a), then the remainder is p(a). This is useful for finding remainders without long division.
examples: Dividing x^2 + 1 by (x − 1): remainder is p(1) = 1^2 + 1 = 2.

concepts.title: Factor Theorem and Factorisation
concepts.definition: Factor Theorem: x-a is a factor of p(x) if p(a)=0. Quadratic polynomials can be factorised by splitting the middle term. Cubic polynomials are factorised by finding one factor via trial and then dividing/splitting.
formulae: Splitting the middle term: For ax² + bx + c, find p, q such that p+q=b and pq=ac.
examples: Example 6: x+2 is a factor of x³+3x²+5x+6 since p(-2) = 0.
Example 8: Factorise 6x² + 17x + 5 = 6x² + 2x + 15x + 5 = (3x + 1)(2x + 5).
Example 10: Factorise x³ - 23x² + 142x - 120 = (x - 1)(x - 10)(x - 12) (found x-1 factor by trial p(1)=0).

concepts.title: Algebraic Identities
concepts.definition: Algebraic equations true for all values of variables. Used for simplification, product expansion, and factorisation.
formulae: I: (x + y)² = x² + 2xy + y²
II: (x - y)² = x² - 2xy + y²
III: x² - y² = (x + y)(x - y)
IV: (x + a)(x + b) = x² + (a + b)x + ab
V: (x + y + z)² = x² + y² + z² + 2xy + 2yz + 2zx
VI: (x + y)³ = x³ + y³ + 3xy(x + y)
VII: (x - y)³ = x³ - y³ - 3xy(x - y)
VIII: x³ + y³ + z³ - 3xyz = (x + y + z)(x² + y² + z² - xy - yz - zx)
examples: Example 13: 49a² + 70ab + 25b² = (7a + 5b)².

concepts.title: Cartesian Plane and Coordinate Axes
concepts.definition: The system for describing the position of a point in a plane using two perpendicular lines, the horizontal x-axis and the vertical y-axis. Intersection is the origin (O). Developed by René Descartes (Cartesian system).
examples: The plane is also called the Cartesian plane, coordinate plane, or xy-plane.
Coordinate axes divide the plane into 4 parts called quadrants.

concepts.title: Coordinates of a Point: Abscissa and Ordinate
concepts.definition: A point is represented by an ordered pair (x, y). The x-coordinate (abscissa) is the perpendicular distance from the y-axis. The y-coordinate (ordinate) is the perpendicular distance from the x-axis.
examples: Example 1: Point B has abscissa 4 and ordinate 3, so its coordinates are (4, 3).
Note: (3, 4) is not the same as (4, 3) unless x = y.
Coordinates of M(-3, 4), L(-5, -4), and S(3, -4) represent unique positions.

concepts.title: Quadrants and Sign Conventions
concepts.definition: The axes divide the plane into four quadrants, numbered I, II, III, IV anticlockwise from the positive x-axis.
examples: Quadrant I: (+, +)
Quadrant II: (-, +)
Quadrant III: (-, -)
Quadrant IV: (+, -)

concepts.title: Points on Axes and the Origin
concepts.definition: Points lying directly on the axes have one coordinate as zero. The origin O has both coordinates zero.
examples: Example 2: Point A(4, 0) lies on x-axis; Point B(0, 3) lies on y-axis.
Any point on x-axis is of form (x, 0).
Any point on y-axis is of form (0, y).
Origin coordinates: (0, 0).

concepts.title: Linear Equations in Two Variables: Standard Form
concepts.definition: An equation that can be put in the form ax + by + c = 0, where a, b and c are real numbers, and a and b are not both zero. This is the standard form of a linear equation in two variables x and y.
formulae: Standard Form: ax + by + c = 0
examples: Example 1: 2x + 3y = 4.37 can be written as 2x + 3y - 4.37 = 0 where a=2, b=3, c=-4.37.
Example 2: x = -5 can be written in two variables as 1x + 0y + 5 = 0.
Equations like 4 - 3x = 0 are also linear equations in two variables: -3x + 0y + 4 = 0.

concepts.title: Solutions of a Linear Equation
concepts.definition: A solution is a pair of values (x, y) that satisfy the equation. A linear equation in two variables has infinitely many solutions. Every solution corresponds to a point on its graph.
examples: Example 3: For x + 2y = 6, four solutions are (6,0), (0,3), (2,2), (4,1).
Example 4: For 4x + 3y = 12, two solutions are (3,0) and (0,4).
Summary points: (i) Infinitely many solutions exist. (ii) Every solution is an ordered pair. (iii) Setting x = 0 or y = 0 is an easy way to find solutions.

concepts.title: Finding Solutions
concepts.definition: Solutions can be found by choosing a value for one variable and solving for the other.
examples: For 3x − y = 5, taking x = 2 gives 6 − y = 5 ⇒ y = 1, so (2,1) is a solution.

concepts.title: History and Euclid's Axioms
concepts.definition: Geometry (Greek 'geo': earth, 'metrein': measure) developed from practical needs like Nile boundary marking and Harappan planning (brick ratio 4:2:1). Euclid collected this knowledge in 'Elements' (13 books). Axioms are universal truths across mathematics.
formulae: Axiom 1: Things equal to the same thing are equal.
Axiom 2 & 3: If equals are added to or subtracted from equals, the results are equal.
Axiom 5: The whole is greater than the part.
examples: History: Indus Valley cities used underground drainage and specific brick ratios.
Axiom 4: Things which coincide with one another are equal (justifies superposition).
Axiom 6 & 7: Doubles or halves of the same things are equal to one another.

concepts.title: Euclid's Definitions and Postulates 1-4
concepts.definition: Euclid listed 23 definitions (e.g., 'Point is that which has no part', 'Line is breadthless length'). Postulates are assumptions specific to geometry. Dimensions: Solid (3), Surface (2), Line (1), Point (0).
formulae: Postulate 1: A straight line may be drawn from any one point to any other point.
Postulate 2: A terminated line can be produced indefinitely.
Postulate 3: A circle can be drawn with any centre and any radius.
Postulate 4: All right angles are equal to one another.
examples: Axiom 5.1: Given two distinct points, there is a unique line that passes through them.
Euclid's Definitions: Ends of lines are points; edges of surfaces are lines.
Terms like 'point', 'line', and 'plane' are now taken as undefined by mathematicians.

concepts.title: Postulate 5, Theorems and Proofs
concepts.definition: The complex Fifth Postulate deals with intersecting lines based on interior angle sums. Statements proved using axioms/postulates are called theorems or propositions.
formulae: Postulate 5: If interior angles on the same side sum to < 180°, lines must eventually intersect on that side.
Theorem 5.1: Two distinct lines cannot have more than one point in common.
examples: Example 1: If B lies between A and C, then AB + BC = AC (Axiom 4).
Example 2: An equilateral triangle can be constructed on any line segment using circles (Postulate 3).
Playfair's Axiom: For every line l and point P not on l, there exists a unique line m through P parallel to l.

concepts.title: Basic Terms, Angle Types and Pairs
concepts.definition: A line segment has two end points; a ray has one. Collinear points lie on the same line. Angles are formed by two rays (arms) originating from a common point (vertex). Different angle types and pairs describe their geometric relationships.
formulae: Acute: 0° < x < 90°; Right: x = 90°; Obtuse: 90° < x < 180°
Straight: x = 180°; Reflex: 180° < x < 360°
Complementary: sum = 90°; Supplementary: sum = 180°
Linear Pair Sum = 180° (Axiom 6.1)
examples: Adjacent angles: Common vertex, common arm, non-common arms on different sides.
Theorem 6.1: If two lines intersect, vertically opposite angles are equal.
Example 1: Lines intersect at O. If ∠POR : ∠ROQ = 5:7, then ∠POR = 75° and ∠ROQ = 105°.
Example 3: Sum of all angles formed by four rays originating from a point is 360°.

concepts.title: Parallel Lines and Transversals
concepts.definition: A transversal is a line that intersects two or more lines at distinct points. Parallel lines are non-intersecting lines where the distance between them (perpendiculars) is constant.
formulae: Corresponding angles are equal if lines are parallel.
Alternate interior/exterior angles are equal if lines are parallel.
Interior angles on the same side of transversal (co-interior) are supplementary.
examples: Theorem 6.6: Lines parallel to the same line are parallel to each other.
Example 4: Parallel lines PQ and RS with transversal XM/MY. If ∠MXQ = 135° and ∠MYR = 40°, then ∠XMY = 85°.
Note: These properties are used for ray diagrams in Science and layout planning in Architecture.

concepts.title: Angle Sum Property of a Triangle (Intro)
concepts.definition: Sum of interior angles of a triangle is 180°, often used with line and angle properties.
examples: If two angles of triangle are 50° and 60°, third angle = 70°.

concepts.title: Congruence of Triangles and Criteria (SAS, ASA, AAS)
concepts.definition: Two triangles are congruent if they are of the same shape and size. Corresponding parts of congruent triangles are equal (CPCT). Criteria ensure congruence using minimal pairs of equal parts.
formulae: SAS Axiom 7.1: Two sides and the included angle are equal.
ASA Theorem 7.1: Two angles and the included side are equal.
AAS Rule: Two angles and one corresponding side are equal (deduced from ASA).
examples: Congruent figures: Two bangles, ATM cards, or coins minted in the same year cover each other completely.
Example 1: In quadrilateral ADBC, OA=OB and OD=OC. By SAS rule, ΔAOD ≅ ΔBOC.
Example 3: AAS Rule holds because if two angles are equal, the third is also equal (Angle Sum Property).

concepts.title: Properties of Isosceles and Equilateral Triangles
concepts.definition: An isosceles triangle has at least two equal sides. An equilateral triangle has all three sides equal. Specific theorems relate equal sides to equal angles.
formulae: Theorem 7.2: Angles opposite to equal sides of an isosceles triangle are equal.
Theorem 7.3: Sides opposite to equal angles of a triangle are equal (Converse).
Each angle of an equilateral triangle is 60°.
examples: Isosceles activity: Fold a cut-out triangle along the angle bisector AD; if AB covers AC, the triangle is isosceles.
Example 4: In ΔABC, if bisector AD is ⊥ side BC, then ΔABD ≅ ΔACD by ASA, so AB = AC (CPCT).
Example 5: If E and F are midpoints of equal sides AB and AC, then BF = CE (SAS proof).

concepts.title: More Criteria: SSS and RHS Congruence
concepts.definition: Additional rules for congruence involving three sides or right-angled triangles with their hypotenuse.
formulae: SSS Theorem 7.4: Three sides of one triangle are equal to three sides of another.
RHS Theorem 7.5: In right triangles, hypotenuse and one side are equal.
examples: Example 7: PQ is perpendicular bisector of AB if P and Q are equidistant from A and B (Proved via SSS and SAS).
RHS Rule: Used in right-angled triangles where the right angle is not the included angle.
Example 8: Point P is equidistant from two intersecting lines l and m if AP bisects the angle between them (RHS rule).

concepts.title: General Properties of Quadrilaterals
concepts.definition: A quadrilateral is a closed four-sided figure with 4 vertices and 4 angles. The sum of the angles of a quadrilateral is 360° (Angle Sum Property).
formulae: Sum of interior angles = 360°
examples: If three angles of a quadrilateral are 110°, 70° and 80°, the fourth angle is 360° - (110°+70°+80°) = 100°.
Quadrilaterals are used in floor patterns and daily objects like table tops.

concepts.title: Parallelogram and Its Properties
concepts.definition: A parallelogram is a quadrilateral where both pairs of opposite sides are parallel. It has unique properties regarding its diagonals, sides, and angles.
formulae: Theorem 8.1: A diagonal divides it into two congruent triangles.
Theorem 8.2 & 8.4: Opposite sides and opposite angles are equal.
Theorem 8.6: Diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other.
examples: Converse Theorems: If each pair of opposite sides is equal (8.3) or opposite angles is equal (8.5), it is a parallelogram.
Theorem 8.7: If diagonals of a quadrilateral bisect each other, it is a parallelogram.

concepts.title: Special Parallelograms: Rectangle, Rhombus, Square
concepts.definition: Special quadrilaterals inherit parallelogram properties but add specific conditions like equal sides or right angles.
formulae: Rectangle: A parallelogram with one right angle (proves all are 90°).
Rhombus: A parallelogram with all sides equal.
Square: A parallelogram with all sides equal and all angles 90°.
examples: Example 1: Show that each angle of a rectangle is a right angle using interior angles property.
Example 2: Diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular to each other.
Square diagonals bisect at right angles and are equal.

concepts.title: The Mid-point Theorem
concepts.definition: A fundamental theorem relating the mid-points of triangle sides to the third side.
formulae: Theorem 8.8: Line segment joining mid-points of two sides is parallel to third side and half of it.
Theorem 8.9: Line through mid-point of one side parallel to another side bisects the third side.
examples: Example 6: Joining mid-points of all sides of ΔABC divides it into four congruent triangles.
Theorem 8.8 application: If E and F are mid-points of AB and AC, then EF || BC and EF = 1/2 BC.

concepts.title: Area of Parallelogram
concepts.definition: Area of a parallelogram equals product of its base and corresponding height.
formulae: Area(parallelogram) = base × height
examples: Parallelogram with base 8 cm and height 5 cm has area 40 cm².

concepts.title: Area of Triangle
concepts.definition: Area of triangle is half the product of its base and corresponding height.
formulae: Area(triangle) = (1/2) × base × height
examples: Triangle with base 6 cm and height 4 cm has area 12 cm².

concepts.title: Triangles on the Same Base and Between Same Parallels
concepts.definition: Triangles on same base and between same parallels have equal area.
examples: Two different triangles on segment AB and between given parallels have equal area.

concepts.title: Basics of Circles and Related Terms
concepts.definition: A circle is the collection of all points in a plane equidistant from a fixed point (centre). Key parts include Radius, Diameter (longest chord), Chord, Arcs (Major/Minor), Segments and Sectors. Points lying on the same circle are called concyclic.
formulae: Circumference = 2πr
Diameter = 2 × Radius
examples: Real-world circles: Wheels, clocks, coins, and bangles.
Segment: Region between a chord and either of its arcs.
Sector: Region between an arc and two radii joining the centre to its endpoints.

concepts.title: Angle Subtended by a Chord
concepts.definition: Chords subtend angles at the centre and at points on the circumference. The relationship depends on the chord length.
formulae: Theorem 9.1: Equal chords subtend equal angles at the centre.
Theorem 9.2 (Converse): If central angles are equal, chords are equal.
examples: Angle POQ: Angle subtended by chord PQ at centre O.
Exercise 9.1 Q1: Equal chords of congruent circles subtend equal angles at their centres.

concepts.title: Perpendicular from Centre to Chord
concepts.definition: The distance from the centre to a chord is established via the shortest perpendicular line segment.
formulae: Theorem 9.3: Perpendicular from centre bisects the chord.
Theorem 9.4 (Converse): Line through centre bisecting chord is perpendicular to it.
examples: Example 1: AD and BC are chords; if OM ⟂ AB and ON ⟂ CD, then OM and ON are distances.
Distance of diameter from centre is zero as the centre lies on it.

concepts.title: Equal Chords and their Distances from Centre
concepts.definition: The length of a chord determines its distance from the centre; longer chord is nearer to the centre.
formulae: Theorem 9.5: Equal chords are equidistant from the centre.
Theorem 9.6: Chords equidistant from the centre are equal in length.
examples: Example 1: If intersecting chords make equal angles with the diameter through their intersection, they are equal.
Exercise 9.2 Q4: Proving AB = CD for a line intersecting concentric circles.

concepts.title: Angle Subtended by an Arc and Segment Properties
concepts.definition: An arc subtends a specific angle at the centre and remaining part of the circle. This leads to the arc-angle relationship.
formulae: Theorem 9.7: Angle at centre is double the angle at any point on the remaining part.
Theorem 9.8: Angles in the same segment of a circle are equal.
Theorem 9.9: Four points are concyclic if a segment subtends equal angles at them.
Angle in a semicircle is a right angle (90°).
examples: Example 2: If chord CD equals radius, angle subtended at major arc is 30°.
Example 4: If AD and AC are diameters, then B lies on segment DC (using semicircle 90° property).

concepts.title: Cyclic Quadrilaterals
concepts.definition: A quadrilateral is cyclic if all its vertices lie on a circle. Its opposite angles have a specific sum relationship.
formulae: Theorem 9.10: Sum of either pair of opposite angles is 180°.
Theorem 9.11 (Converse): If opposite angles sum to 180°, it is cyclic.
examples: Example 3: In cyclic quadrilateral ABCD, if ∠DBC = 55° and ∠BAC = 45°, then ∠BCD = 80°.
Example 5: Quad formed by internal angle bisectors of any quadrilateral is cyclic.
Theorem: A cyclic parallelogram is a rectangle.

concepts.title: Basic Constructions
concepts.definition: Using ruler and compass to construct line segments, perpendiculars and angle bisectors following standard steps.
examples: Constructing perpendicular bisector of a given line segment.

concepts.title: Constructing Triangles
concepts.definition: Triangles can be constructed given suitable data like SSS, SAS, ASA using compass and straightedge.
examples: Construct triangle ABC given AB, BC and AC (SSS construction).

concepts.title: Constructions Using Given Angles
concepts.definition: Angles of specific measures are constructed and used to form triangles and other figures.
examples: Constructing a triangle when two angles and the included side are known.

concepts.title: Heron's Formula and Historical Context
concepts.definition: Heron's formula (or Hero's formula) calculates the area of a triangle using its three sides. It was derived by Heron of Alexandria (born ~10 AD), an encyclopedic writer in applied mathematics and mensuration.
formulae: Area = √[s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)]
Semi-perimeter (s) = (a + b + c) / 2
examples: Example 1: Sides 8 cm, 11 cm, perimeter 32 cm. Semi-perimeter s = 16. Area = √(16×8×5×3) = 8√30 cm².
Formula derivation: Found in Book I of Heron's geometrical works, which also deals with squares, rectangles, triangles, and trapezoids.

concepts.title: Heron’s Formula for Area of Triangle
concepts.definition: Heron’s formula is particularly useful when the height of the triangle is unknown. It gives area in terms of three sides and semi‑perimeter s.
formulae: Area = √[s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)]
examples: Example 2: Triangular park with sides 120m, 80m, 50m. s = 125m. Area = 375√15 m². Fencing cost (₹20/m, 3m gate) = ₹4940.
Isosceles check: Sides 5, 5, 8. s = 9. Area = √(9×1×4×4) = 12 cm².
Equilateral check: Side 10. s = 15. Area = √(15×5×5×5) = 25√3 cm².

concepts.title: Applications and Mensuration of Quadrilaterals
concepts.definition: Heron's formula can be applied to quadrilaterals by dividing them into two triangles. Heron's works deal largely with mensuration of specialised quadrilaterals and trapezoids.
examples: Example 3: Ratio of sides 3:5:7, perimeter 300m. Sides are 60m, 100m, 140m. s = 150. Area = 1500√3 m².
Mensuration: Heron's 'Book I' covers areas of trapezoids (trapezia), various specialised quadrilaterals, regular polygons and circles.

concepts.title: Surface Area of Cube and Cuboid
concepts.definition: Surface area is sum of areas of all faces of a solid; for cube and cuboid there are direct formulas.
formulae: Total surface area (cube) = 6a²
Total surface area (cuboid) = 2(lb + bh + hl)
examples: Cube of side 4 cm has surface area 6 × 16 = 96 cm².

concepts.title: Surface Area of Right Circular Cylinder and Cone
concepts.definition: Curved and total surface areas of these solids are calculated based on their principal dimensions. A right circular cone is generated by rotating a right triangle about one of its perpendicular sides.
formulae: Curved SA (cylinder) = 2πrh
Total SA (cylinder) = 2πrh + 2πr²
Slant height of cone (l) = √(r² + h²)
Curved SA (cone) = πrl
Total SA (cone) = πrl + πr² = πr(l + r)
examples: Cylinder with r = 3 cm, h = 7 cm: curved area = 2π × 3 × 7 = 42π cm².
Example 3: Corn cob (cone-shaped) with radius 2.1 cm and height 20 cm. Curved area calculation used to find number of grains (~531 total grains at 4 per cm²).

concepts.title: Volumes of Cube, Cuboid and Cylinder
concepts.definition: Volume is measure of space occupied; formulas depend on base area and height.
formulae: Volume (cube) = a³
Volume (cuboid) = l × b × h
Volume (cylinder) = πr²h
examples: Cuboid 5 × 3 × 2 cm has volume 30 cm³.

concepts.title: Surface Area of Sphere and Hemisphere
concepts.definition: A sphere is a three-dimensional figure made of points equidistant from a fixed centre. A hemisphere is exactly half of a sphere sliced through the middle.
formulae: Surface Area of Sphere = 4πr²
Curved SA of Hemisphere = 2πr²
Total SA of Hemisphere = 3πr²
examples: Example 7: Hemispherical dome of building (circumference 17.6 m) needs painting. CSA = 49.28 m². Cost at ₹5 per 100 cm² (~₹500/m²) is ₹24640.
Sphere surface area is equal to the area of four circles of the same radius.

concepts.title: Volume of a Right Circular Cone
concepts.definition: The volume of a cone is exactly one-third the volume of a cylinder which has the same base radius and height.
formulae: Volume of Cone = (1/3)πr²h
examples: Example 9: Conical tent with radius 7m and canvas area 551 m² (1 m² wastage). CSA = 550 m², slant height l = 25m, height h = 24m, Volume = 1232 m³.
Filling experiment: Three full cones of sand fill exactly one cylinder of same r and h.

concepts.title: Volume of Sphere and Hemisphere
concepts.definition: The volume of a sphere depends on its radius cubed. Volume is the measure of region in space, while capacity is the volume of substance that a hollow container can hold.
formulae: Volume of Sphere = (4/3)πr³
Volume of Hemisphere = (2/3)πr³
examples: Example 11: Metallic shot-putt (sphere) with radius 4.9 cm and density 7.8 g/cm³. Volume ~493 cm³, Mass ~3.85 kg.
Example 12: Hemispherical bowl with radius 3.5 cm contains ~89.8 cm³ of water.

concepts.title: Graphical Representation of Data
concepts.definition: Visual representation makes data easier to understand. Methods include Bar Graphs, Histograms, and Frequency Polygons.
formulae: Class-mark = (Upper limit + Lower limit) / 2
Modified Length (Varying Histogram) = (Frequency / Width) × Minimum Class Size
examples: Bar Graph: Used for variables like 'month of birth' (Example 1) or 'monthly expenditure heads' (Example 2) using uniform width bars and equal gaps.
Histogram: Used for continuous class intervals (e.g., weights). Areas of rectangles are proportional to frequencies.
Histogram (Varying Width): If class sizes vary, rectangle lengths must be modified (e.g., Example 3 where 0-20 interval length is adjusted based on minimum size 10).
Frequency Polygon: Formed by joining mid-points (class-marks) of the upper sides of histogram rectangles or plotted individually (Example 5).

concepts.title: Mean, Median and Mode of Ungrouped Data
concepts.definition: Mean is arithmetic average, median is middle value in ordered data, mode is most frequent value.
formulae: Mean = (sum of observations) / (number of observations)
examples: For data 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, mean = 6; median = 6; if 4 occurs most, mode = 4.

concepts.title: Measures of Central Tendency (Basic Interpretation)
concepts.definition: Mean, median and mode summarise data using a single representative value, each with different uses and sensitivities to extreme values.
examples: Mean affected by very high or low values; median more robust to outliers.

concepts.title: Random Experiment and Sample Space
concepts.definition: A random experiment is an action whose outcomes cannot be predicted with certainty but all possible outcomes are known; set of all outcomes is sample space.
examples: Tossing a coin has sample space {H, T}; rolling a die has {1,2,3,4,5,6}.

concepts.title: Event and Favourable Outcomes
concepts.definition: An event is a subset of sample space. Favourable outcomes are those outcomes that belong to the event in question.
examples: Event ‘getting even number’ when rolling a die has favourable outcomes {2,4,6}.

concepts.title: Empirical Probability (Experimental)
concepts.definition: Empirical probability of an event is estimated from experiments as the ratio of number of times the event occurs to total number of trials.
formulae: P(E) ≈ (number of times E occurs) / (total number of trials)
examples: If a coin is tossed 100 times and shows head 55 times, empirical probability of head is 55/100 = 0.55.
